content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Every developer has built a todo app at some point.
I wanted to build something that solved a real problem instead.
As a volunteer working on community activities, we often have internal meetings to discuss upcoming events, assign responsibilities, and coordinate with the team. We usually relied on existing meeting platforms, but for our small group, they often felt like overkill.
That made me wonder:
What if I built a meeting app that only included the features we actually needed?
That question became the starting point of this project.
Keeping the Stack Simple
One decision I made early was to avoid using a frontend framework.
Not because React or Vue aren’t great. I use React regularly.
For this project, I wanted to understand what was happening under the hood.
So the stack looked like this:

• HTML

• CSS

• Vanilla JavaScript

• WebRTC

• Progressive Web App
Using plain JavaScript forced me to interact directly with browser APIs instead of relying on abstractions. It made the learning process much more rewarding.
Understanding WebRTC Through Building
Before this project, WebRTC always felt like one of those technologies that looked intimidating from a distance.
There are terms like ICE candidates, SDP, signaling, and peer connections that can make the learning curve seem steep.
The best way I found to understand it was by building something useful.
For our meetings, I chose a mesh topology.
Each participant creates a direct peer-to-peer connection with every other participant.
For a small community meeting with only a handful of people, this approach works well because it keeps the architecture simple.
Of course, I also learned where it starts to break down. As the number of participants grows, the number of connections increases rapidly. That made me appreciate why larger meeting platforms use SFUs or MCUs instead of a mesh network.
Sometimes understanding the limitations of an approach is just as valuable as understanding its strengths.
Small Features That Teach Big Concepts
One feature I enjoyed implementing was audio controls.
Muting a microphone isn’t about disconnecting from the meeting.
It’s simply enabling or disabling the audio track.
const audioTrack = localStream.getAudioTracks()[0];
audioTrack.enabled = !audioTrack.enabled;It’s a small piece of code, but writing it helped me understand how media streams actually work inside the browser.
Another feature I experimented with was a background changer.
I wasn’t trying to recreate premium video conferencing software.
I simply wanted to explore how video streams could be processed before being shared with other participants.
Projects become much more interesting when every feature is an opportunity to learn something new.
Making It Feel Like a Native App
Since the application was meant for repeated internal use, I decided to turn it into a Progressive Web App.
Adding a web app manifest and a service worker didn’t require much code, but the experience improved immediately.
The app could be installed directly from the browser, launched from the home screen, and continue working even when parts of it were cached offline.
It’s one of those improvements that users appreciate without even thinking about it.
The Biggest Lesson
The most valuable part of this project wasn’t learning another API.
It was realizing that the best learning projects come from solving your own problems.
When you’re building something people around you will actually use, every decision feels more meaningful.
You think more carefully about usability.
You debug with more patience.
You care more about performance.
And in the end, you don’t just have another portfolio project. You have a tool that provides real value.
What’s Next?
This project also opened up a lot of interesting areas I want to explore further.

• Better signaling architecture

• Screen sharing

• Recording meetings

• Replacing mesh networking with an SFU for larger groups

• Improving the PWA experience with smarter caching strategies
Each improvement is another opportunity to learn something new.
Final Thoughts
Looking back, I’m glad I chose to build this instead of another demo application.
It taught me more about browser APIs, peer-to-peer networking, and Progressive Web Apps than any tutorial could.
More importantly, it reminded me that some of the best projects don’t start with a new technology.
They start with a simple question:
“What problem do I keep running into, and can I build something to solve it?”
That’s the kind of project worth sharing, and the kind of project that teaches the most.
Project Link — https://github.com/KalpickSharma/meet-prototype.git
